_provenance.stage: Stage 1 — derived from live cards for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: Expat Salary Floor Doubles to RM20K; The Talent Equation Nobody Solves
context: From 1 June 2026, Employment Pass Category I minimum rises from RM10,000 to RM20,000. Category III capped at 5 years with mandatory succession planning. The policy aims to prioritise local talent, but the talent pipeline, brain drain, and enforcement gaps remain unaddressed.

cards.text: Malaysia doubles the expat salary floor to RM20,000. The message: hire Malaysians first.
cards.sub: RM10,000 was too low and undercut locals. A higher floor can attract quality investment and force knowledge transfer. But is the system ready?

cards.h: Semiconductor target: 60,000 workers by 2030. Pipeline: 3,000 graduates/year. Many leave for Singapore within five years.
cards.s: Malaysia loses skilled professionals to neighbours offering higher pay and clearer meritocracy. Raising the price of foreign replacements doesn't fix why Malaysians leave.
cards.d: Semiconductor target: 60,000 workers by 2030. Pipeline: 3,000 graduates/year. Many leave for Singapore within five years. Malaysia loses skilled professionals to neighbours offering higher pay and clearer meritocracy. Raising the price of foreign replacements doesn't fix why Malaysians leave.

cards.h: GLCs and MNCs absorb the RM20K floor easily. SMEs and startups (the actual growth engine) cannot.
cards.s: A Penang semiconductor startup now faces double the floor for a foreign engineer. Succession planning is mandatory on paper but rarely enforced. The burden falls hardest on firms least able to bear it.
cards.d: GLCs and MNCs absorb the RM20K floor easily. SMEs and startups (the actual growth engine) cannot. A Penang semiconductor startup now faces double the floor for a foreign engineer. Succession planning is mandatory on paper but rarely enforced. The burden falls hardest on firms least able to bear it.

cards.h: Malaysia's new RM20K floor exceeds Singapore's EP minimum of SGD5,000 (~RM16,600).
cards.s: Multinationals choosing between Penang and Batam now see Malaysia as the more expensive jurisdiction. Thailand and Vietnam offer alternatives with lower barriers.
cards.d: Malaysia's new RM20K floor exceeds Singapore's EP minimum of SGD5,000 (~RM16,600). Multinationals choosing between Penang and Batam now see Malaysia as the more expensive jurisdiction. Thailand and Vietnam offer alternatives with lower barriers.

cards.text: The real talent equation: pipeline quality, brain drain, and enforcement. This policy moves none. It adjusts the price tag on a problem that is not about price.
Protecting Malaysian jobs is right. But protection without pipeline investment produces paper compliance, not specialists. The ladder must come before the bar.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
